questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What are the products of the complete combustion of propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$)?

choices.explanation: Complete combustion of any hydrocarbon with excess $\text{O}_2$ produces $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ only. Balanced: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{(g)} + 5\text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2\text{(g)} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$.
$\text{CO}$ is produced only in incomplete combustion (insufficient $\text{O}_2$); complete combustion produces $\text{CO}_2$.
Carbon soot forms only in very oxygen-limited combustion; complete combustion with excess $\text{O}_2$ produces $\text{CO}_2$, not $\text{C(s)}$.
Hydrogen gas is not a combustion product; complete combustion of hydrocarbons produces water, not $\text{H}_2$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In the reaction $\text{Zn(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}$, zinc is being:

choices.explanation: Zinc changes from $\text{Zn}^0$ to $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, losing 2 electrons; this is oxidation, not reduction.
Zinc's oxidation state changes from 0 to +2, meaning it loses 2 electrons; this is oxidation. Zinc acts as the reducing agent.
The oxidizing agent is the species that gains electrons; here $\text{H}^+$ is reduced (0 → 0 from +1), making $\text{H}^+$ the oxidizing agent.
Zinc undergoes oxidation from $\text{Zn}^0$ to $\text{Zn}^{2+}$; it is actively involved in electron transfer, not a spectator.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the net ionic equation for the reaction between $\text{AgNO}_3\text{(aq)}$ and $\text{NaCl(aq)}$?

choices.explanation: This is the complete molecular equation; the net ionic equation removes spectator ions ($\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$).
The driving force is precipitation of $\text{AgCl}$; $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ are spectator ions and are omitted from the net ionic equation.
$\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ are spectator ions; they appear on both sides and are canceled from the net ionic equation.
Silver does not bond with sodium; the precipitation involves $\text{Ag}^+$ combining with $\text{Cl}^-$, not $\text{Na}^+$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: How many grams of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ are produced when 5.00 mol of $\text{H}_2$ reacts completely with excess $\text{O}_2$? ($2\text{H}_2\text{(g)} + \text{O}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$; molar mass $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ = 18.0 g/mol)

choices.explanation: 45.0 g = 2.50 mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, which uses a 2:1 H₂:H₂O ratio instead of the 1:1 ratio in the balanced equation.
The balanced equation shows 2 mol $\text{H}_2$ → 2 mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (1:1 ratio); 5.00 mol $\text{H}_2$ → 5.00 mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$; mass $= 5.00 \times 18.0 = 90.0$ g.
180 g = 10.0 mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, which would require 10.0 mol $\text{H}_2$, not 5.00 mol.
36.0 g = 2.00 mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$; the correct 1:1 stoichiometry gives 5.00 mol $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In the reaction $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\text{(s)} + 3\text{CO(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe(s)} + 3\text{CO}_2\text{(g)}$, what is the oxidation state of iron in $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$?

choices.explanation: In $\text{FeO}$, iron is +2; in $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$, two Fe atoms must balance three O atoms (each −2), giving $2x + 3(-2) = 0$; $x = +3$.
In $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$: $2(\text{OS of Fe}) + 3(-2) = 0$; $2x = +6$; $x = +3$. Iron is reduced from +3 to 0 in the products.
Oxidation state 0 applies to elemental iron $\text{Fe(s)}$; in $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ (an ionic compound), iron has a positive oxidation state.
Oxygen has oxidation state −2 in compounds like $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$; iron is positive.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A student reacts 4.00 g of $\text{H}_2$ with excess $\text{O}_2$ and collects 32.0 g of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. The theoretical yield is 36.0 g. What is the percent yield?

choices.explanation: Percent yield cannot exceed 100%; 112.5% would indicate more product than theoretically possible.
75.0% would give an actual yield of $0.75 \times 36.0 = 27.0$ g; the actual yield here is 32.0 g.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What is the limiting reagent when 3.0 mol $\text{N}_2$ and 6.0 mol $\text{H}_2$ react according to $\text{N}_2\text{(g)} + 3\text{H}_2\text{(g)} \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3\text{(g)}$?

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$, because there are fewer moles of it
choices.explanation: Fewer moles does not necessarily mean the limiting reagent; stoichiometric ratios must be compared.

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$, because the reaction requires 3× as much $\text{H}_2$ as $\text{N}_2$
choices.explanation: 3.0 mol $\text{N}_2$ would require $3.0 \times 3 = 9.0$ mol $\text{H}_2$, but only 6.0 mol $\text{H}_2$ is available; $\text{H}_2$ runs out first and is the limiting reagent.
Products cannot be limiting reagents; limiting reagents are reactants.

choices.text: Both reagents are consumed equally; there is no limiting reagent
choices.explanation: The stoichiometric ratio is $1:3$ ($\text{N}_2$:$\text{H}_2$); the available ratio is $3:6 = 1:2$, which does not match the 1:3 requirement, so $\text{H}_2$ is limiting.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: What volume of 0.500 M $\text{NaOH}$ is needed to neutralize 25.0 mL of 0.300 M $\text{HCl}$? ($\text{NaOH} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, 1:1 ratio)

choices.explanation: Equal volumes are needed only when concentrations are equal; here NaOH (0.500 M) is more concentrated than HCl (0.300 M), so less volume of NaOH is needed.
41.7 mL would result from incorrectly using HCl as the titrant of NaOH; the correct calculation gives 15.0 mL NaOH.
Using the correct formula: $V_{\text{NaOH}} = M_{\text{HCl}} \times V_{\text{HCl}} / M_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.300 \times 25.0 / 0.500 = 15.0$ mL.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Balance the following redox reaction in acidic solution: $\text{MnO}_4^-\text{(aq)} + \text{Fe}^{2+}\text{(aq)} \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+}\text{(aq)} + \text{Fe}^{3+}\text{(aq)}$. What are the coefficients for $\text{MnO}_4^-$ and $\text{Fe}^{2+}$?

choices.explanation: Mn gains 5 electrons (Mn: +7 → +2); Fe loses 1 electron (Fe: +2 → +3); to balance, need 5 Fe²⁺ per 1 MnO₄⁻.

Mn is reduced: +7 → +2 (gain of 5e⁻). Fe is oxidized: +2 → +3 (loss of 1e⁻ each). To balance electrons: 1 MnO₄⁻ × 5e⁻ = 5 Fe²⁺ × 1e⁻. Full balanced: $\text{MnO}_4^- + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 8\text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{3+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
2 MnO₄⁻ would transfer 10 electrons total; 5 Fe²⁺ only provides 5 electrons; the ratio is 1:5, not 2:5.
This inverts the ratio; 5 Mn × 5e⁻ = 25e⁻ but only 1 Fe provides 1e⁻ — electrons are not balanced.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following reactions is classified as a double displacement (double replacement) reaction?

choices.explanation: This is a synthesis (combination) reaction — two reactants forming one product.
This is a single displacement reaction — zinc replaces copper in the sulfate compound.
Two ionic compounds exchange partners (Ba²⁺ pairs with SO₄²⁻ and Na⁺ pairs with Cl⁻); this is a double displacement (metathesis) reaction resulting in precipitation.
This is a decomposition reaction — a single compound breaks down into simpler substances.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A chemist dissolves 29.2 g of $\text{NaCl}$ (molar mass 58.5 g/mol) in enough water to make 500.0 mL of solution. What is the molarity?

choices.explanation: 2.00 M would require 1.00 mol NaCl (58.5 g); only 29.2 g (0.500 mol) is dissolved.
0.250 M corresponds to 0.125 mol in 500 mL; the calculation gives 0.500 mol → 1.00 M.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which is NOT a spectator ion in the reaction: $\text{Pb(NO}_3)_2\text{(aq)} + 2\text{KI(aq)} \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2\text{(s)} + 2\text{KNO}_3\text{(aq)}$?

choices.explanation: $\text{K}^+$ appears on both sides as $\text{KI(aq)}$ and $\text{KNO}_3\text{(aq)}$; it does not change and is a spectator ion.
$\text{NO}_3^-$ appears in $\text{Pb(NO}_3)_2\text{(aq)}$ and $\text{KNO}_3\text{(aq)}$ on both sides; it is a spectator ion.
$\text{Pb}^{2+}$ combines with $\text{I}^-$ to form the precipitate $\text{PbI}_2\text{(s)}$; it is actively involved in the reaction, not a spectator ion.
$\text{Pb}^{2+}$ and $\text{I}^-$ combine to form the precipitate and are not spectators.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is soluble in water according to solubility rules?

choices.explanation: $\text{AgCl}$ is insoluble ($K_{sp} = 1.8 \times 10^{-10}$); the silver halide rule: $\text{AgCl}$, $\text{AgBr}$, $\text{AgI}$ are insoluble.
$\text{BaSO}_4$ is insoluble — the barium sulfate exception to the soluble sulfates rule.
All sodium compounds are soluble; all nitrate compounds are soluble; $\text{NaNO}_3$ is highly soluble by both rules.
$\text{CaCO}_3$ is insoluble — carbonates are generally insoluble except with Group 1 metals and ammonium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In a redox reaction, the species that is oxidized has its oxidation state:

choices.explanation: A decrease in oxidation state means electrons were gained, which is reduction, not oxidation.
Oxidation is the loss of electrons; losing electrons means fewer negative charges remain on the atom, so the oxidation state increases (becomes more positive).
If oxidation state doesn't change, no electron transfer occurred for that species; it is neither oxidized nor reduced.
While oxidation state can change sign, the key definition is that oxidation increases the oxidation state; the direction of sign change depends on the specific reaction.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: 25.00 mL of an unknown $\text{HCl}$ solution requires 31.20 mL of 0.1500 M $\text{NaOH}$ to reach the equivalence point. What is the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ solution?

choices.explanation: Equal concentrations would require equal volumes; 31.20 mL NaOH ≠ 25.00 mL HCl, so concentrations differ.
Moles NaOH = $0.03120 \times 0.1500 = 0.004680$ mol. At equivalence, moles HCl = 0.004680 mol. $M_{\text{HCl}} = 0.004680/0.02500 = 0.1872$ M.
0.1205 M is not obtained by the correct formula; $M_{\text{HCl}} = (0.03120 \times 0.1500)/0.02500 = 0.1872$ M.
0.2000 M would require moles of HCl = 0.005000 mol, meaning volume of NaOH would be 33.33 mL, not 31.20 mL.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: 500.0 mL of 6.00 M $\text{HCl}$ must be diluted to make a 1.50 M solution. What final volume is needed?

choices.explanation: 125 mL would give a more concentrated solution; using $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$: $V_2 = (6.00 \times 500.0)/1.50 = 2000$ mL.
500 mL is the original volume; the final volume must be larger since concentration is decreasing.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student is given an unknown metal M that reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to produce a colorless gas and a clear solution. A second student burns magnesium ribbon in air and obtains a bright white solid.

questions.question: Which type of reaction produces the colorless gas when metal M reacts with HCl?

choices.explanation: Decomposition produces simpler substances from a single reactant; the metal-acid reaction involves two reactants.
Double displacement involves two ionic compounds exchanging partners; a metal reacting with an acid is single displacement.
Metal M displaces hydrogen from HCl: $\text{M(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}$; this is a single replacement/redox reaction; the gas is $\text{H}_2$.
Combustion requires reaction with oxygen gas and produces $\text{CO}_2$ and/or $\text{H}_2\text{O}$; this metal-HCl reaction produces $\text{H}_2$ gas.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The empirical formula of a compound is $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ and its molar mass is 180 g/mol. What is its molecular formula?

choices.explanation: $\text{CH}_2\text{O}$ has a molar mass of $12 + 2 + 16 = 30$ g/mol; 180/30 = 6, so the molecular formula is 6 times the empirical formula.
$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_2$ has molar mass $2(12) + 4(1) + 2(16) = 60$ g/mol; the target is 180 g/mol.
Empirical formula mass = 30 g/mol; $n = 180/30 = 6$; molecular formula = $6 \times \text{CH}_2\text{O} = \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$ (glucose).
$\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$ has molar mass 90 g/mol ($n = 3$); the required $n$ is 6 for molar mass 180.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: In a disproportionation reaction, a single substance is simultaneously oxidized AND reduced. Which of the following is a disproportionation reaction?

choices.explanation: Here Zn is oxidized and Cu²⁺ is reduced — two different species, not a disproportionation.

In $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$, oxygen is −1. In the products: O in $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is −2 (reduced) and O in $\text{O}_2$ is 0 (oxidized). The same species ($\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$) is both oxidized and reduced — classic disproportionation.
This is a neutralization reaction; no oxidation states change.
This is a synthesis/combustion reaction where Mg is oxidized and O₂ is reduced — two different species, not disproportionation.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a synthesis (combination) reaction?

choices.explanation: This is a decomposition reaction — a single compound breaks down into two simpler products.
This is a double displacement/acid-carbonate reaction with multiple products; not a synthesis reaction.
Two reactants combine to form one product — this is the definition of a synthesis (combination) reaction.
This is a single displacement reaction — iron replaces copper in the sulfate compound.
